content.text: Does Mantine have a way of using CSS clamp() in its themes?

input.command: grep -rl "clamp" node_modules/@mantine/core/styles.css node_modules/@mantine/core/lib 2>/dev/null | head -20; echo "---"; grep -rn "clamp" node_modules/@mantine/*/esm/index.mjs 2>/dev/null | head -20
input.description: Search Mantine package for built-in clamp() usage
Check for CSS clamp() usage in Mantine core styles

content.text: So am I barking up the wrong tree? Is the Mantine-native way of making text fit smaller layouts to us fz breakpoints?

input.command: grep -n "base:" node_modules/@mantine/core/lib/core/Box/style-props/*.d.ts 2>/dev/null | head -5; echo "---"; find node_modules/@mantine/core -iname "*responsive*" | head -10
input.description: Confirm Mantine's responsive style prop mechanism

content.text:
Confirmed — `has-responsive-styles` is real, that's the mechanism. Yes, you're on the more native path with breakpoint objects. Quick comparison:

**Responsive `fz` objects — the idiomatic Mantine way**
```tsx
<Text fz={{ base: 'sm', sm: 'md', lg: 'xl' }}>...</Text>
```
- Uses Mantine's built-in responsive style-props system (`has-responsive-styles` under `Box`), keyed off `theme.breakpoints`
- Compiles to real CSS media queries at build/render time — no JS measuring, no hydration mismatch, works with SSR
- This is the documented, first-class pattern used throughout Mantine's own docs for every style prop (`w`, `p`, `display`, `fz`, etc.), not just typography
- Discrete steps only — text jumps at each breakpoint rather than scaling continuously

**`clamp()` in theme/style props — works, but not a "Mantine feature"**
- Just a raw CSS string Mantine passes through untouched
- Gives smooth, continuous fluid scaling between a min and max, no jump points
- No integration with `theme.breakpoints`, no special support/validation — it's an escape hatch, not something Mantine models

So: not wrong to want fluid text, but if "Mantine-native" means "the mechanism Mantine actually built for this," that's the `fz={{ base, sm, md, ... }}` breakpoint object. Reach for `clamp()` only when you specifically want continuous scaling instead of discrete steps — otherwise breakpoints are simpler, more consistent with the rest of the codebase's responsive props, and easier for other contributors to read.
That suggests that I'm fighting against Mantine's way of doing things by using container queries and having a nav bar which gets hidden on narrower viewports

input.description: Search for existing container query usage in the codebase

content.text:
Good news: your current navbar collapse (`AppShell.Navbar` with `breakpoint: 'sm'`, `hiddenFrom="sm"` on the `Burger`) is already the Mantine-native, viewport-based pattern — not container queries at all. So on that specific component you're not fighting anything.

The real question is whether that generalizes to wherever you were reaching for container queries (I don't see `@container`/`containerType` used anywhere in [src](src) besides an unrelated debug helper). My take: it depends on *why* that element's width would ever differ from the viewport width.

- If the nav (or whatever component) always spans full viewport width, container queries buy you nothing — Mantine's viewport breakpoints (`hiddenFrom`, `visibleFrom`, responsive `{ base, sm, md }` props) already do the job and match the rest of the codebase.
- If it's nested inside something that can be narrower than the viewport (a sidebar, a split panel, an embedded widget reused at different widths), viewport breakpoints genuinely can't express "this shrank because its container shrank, not the window" — that's exactly the case container queries exist for, and Mantine has no native answer to it.

So: not inherently wrong, just situational. Is there a specific component where the container is narrower than the viewport, or is this about the top-level nav bar in [CustomAppShell.tsx](src/components/CustomAppShell/CustomAppShell.tsx)?
I have widths based on cqw in many places

input.description: Find all files using container query length units
Show cqw usage lines in relevant files

result.output:
src/components/PlayerCard/PlayerCard.module.css:19:    max-width: 30cqw;
src/components/PlayerTrophyTally/PlayerTrophyTally.tsx:13:    /** Sizing is the caller's concern — e.g. `cqw` units only make sense in a
src/components/PlayerTrophyTally/PlayerTrophyTally.tsx:42:                    <Flex direction="row" gap="1cqw" align="center">
src/components/CountryFlag/CountryFlag.tsx:6:    /** Sizing is the caller's concern — e.g. `cqw` units only make sense in a
src/components/ClubBadge/ClubBadge.tsx:6:    /** Sizing is the caller's concern — e.g. `cqw` units only make sense in a

input.description: Find container-type declarations establishing container query contexts

content.text:
That reinforces it was the right call, not a fight. `CountryFlag`, `ClubBadge`, and `PlayerTrophyTally` are reusable leaf components dropped into different-width contexts (a card cell, a table cell, a list) — they have no idea what viewport the page is at, only how much box they've been given, which is precisely what `cqw` + `container-type: inline-size` (as set in [PlayerCard.module.css:9](src/components/PlayerCard/PlayerCard.module.css:9)) solves and viewport breakpoints structurally can't.

So you've actually got two separate, correctly-separated concerns: viewport breakpoints (`hiddenFrom`, `visibleFrom`, responsive `fz`/`w` objects) for page-level chrome like the nav bar, and container queries for self-contained components that get reused at arbitrary widths. Keep both — the earlier nav bar question and this one aren't the same problem, and Mantine's viewport-breakpoint system was never meant to cover the second.

Right, but given the Mantine breakpoints are always based on viewport widths, it makes it hard to be consistent because the _container_ widths can vary wildly depending on whether the nav is visible: the font sizes and the container widths can jump at different points in the viewport width spectrum.

You've spotted a real desync: `AppShell.Main`'s actual content width is `viewport − navWidth` when the nav is pinned open, but `fz={{ base, sm, md }}`-style breakpoints only ever see raw viewport width, so the same breakpoint band maps to different real estate depending on nav state — a `md` viewport jump doesn't mean the same available width in both cases.

Given that, and that you've already proven the pattern works (`PlayerCard`, `CountryFlag`, etc.), the more consistent fix is to push `container-type: inline-size` up onto `AppShell.Main`'s content wrapper itself and drive in-content typography/spacing off `cqi`/`cqw` there too, rather than Mantine's viewport `fz` props — that way sizing tracks the space actually left after the nav, not the window. Keep viewport breakpoints only for the one decision that's genuinely about the window — whether there's room for a pinned sidebar at all (the `Burger`/`collapsed` toggle) — everything sized *within* the content area should follow its container, not the viewport.

Trade-off: this means nesting a container context fairly high in the tree (one extra wrapper, one more thing to remember when adding new pages), but it removes the class of bug you just described entirely, and it's consistent with the sizing model you've already committed to elsewhere.
So then I'd have cqw font sizes in the theme?

Yes, and it works better than you might expect, because of how CSS custom properties resolve — but there's one real landmine to guard against.

Mantine's `theme.fontSizes` values just become CSS custom properties (`--mantine-font-size-md: ...`). The string isn't evaluated where it's *declared*, it's substituted lazily wherever it's *used* — so a `cqi`-based value in the theme will resolve against whatever container context happens to be nearest to the element consuming it at render time. Concretely:

```ts
fontSizes: {
  md: 'clamp(1rem, 0.9rem + 0.5cqi, 1.25rem)',
}
```

Anything rendered inside `AppShell.Main`'s content wrapper picks up sizing relative to that container's width. Anything rendered inside `PlayerCard` (which sets its own `container-type: inline-size`) picks up sizing relative to *its* box instead, since `cqi` always resolves against the **nearest** ancestor establishing containment — there's no way to name-target a further-out one for the unit itself (only `@container` at-rules support named targeting, not the length units). So nested containers naturally get their own local scale, which is probably what you want for self-contained widgets like `PlayerCard`.

The footgun: per spec, if an element has **no** container-context ancestor at all, `cqi`/`cqw` compute to a definite `0`, not an error. That's why the `clamp()` form matters — write it as `rem-floor + N·cqi`, not bare `cqi`, so anywhere outside a container context (modals rendered via portal, a page that bypasses `CustomAppShell`, print styles) silently falls back to the rem floor instead of collapsing to zero-size text.

So: yes, theme-level `cqi` font sizes, always wrapped in `clamp()` with rem bounds, never bare.
Add an Obsidian note for this: I need to keep thinking about it.

content.text: There's already a [Mantine.md](/Users/toastboy/obsidian/toastboy/Home%20Lab/New%20Footy%20Website/Mantine.md) note in that folder — let me check it before deciding whether to append or create a new note.

input.file_path: /Users/toastboy/obsidian/toastboy/Home Lab/New Footy Website/Responsive sizing - breakpoints vs container queries.md
input.content:
Open question — needs more thought. Not decided yet.

## The problem

[[Mantine]]'s built-in responsive system (`fz={{ base, sm, md }}`, `theme.breakpoints`, `hiddenFrom`/`visibleFrom`) is all **viewport**-width based.

But `CustomAppShell`'s `AppShell.Main` content width isn't the viewport width — it's `viewport − navWidth` whenever the nav is pinned open (above the `sm` breakpoint). So the same Mantine viewport breakpoint band maps to different actual content widths depending on nav state. Font sizes and layout driven by viewport `fz` breakpoints can jump at the "wrong" points relative to how much space the content area actually has.

Meanwhile several reusable leaf components already use CSS container queries instead, sized relative to whatever box they're dropped into rather than the viewport:

- `CountryFlag`, `ClubBadge`, `PlayerTrophyTally` — sized with `cqw`
- `PlayerCard.module.css` sets `container-type: inline-size` and uses `max-width: 30cqw`

That part is solid — it's the right tool for self-contained components reused at arbitrary widths (card cell vs table cell vs list).

## The harder question

Should the *page content area* (inside `AppShell.Main`) also move to container queries, to avoid the viewport/content-width mismatch entirely?

Sketch of the idea:
- Set `container-type: inline-size` on `AppShell.Main`'s content wrapper.
- Drive in-content typography/spacing off `cqi` rather than Mantine's viewport `fz` props, so sizing tracks the space actually left after the nav, not the window.
- Keep viewport breakpoints only for the one decision that's genuinely about the window: whether there's room for a pinned sidebar at all (the `Burger`/`collapsed` toggle in `CustomAppShell.tsx`).

Theme-level idea if this goes ahead — `theme.fontSizes` values are just CSS custom property strings, resolved lazily at point of use, so they can reference `cqi`:

```ts
fontSizes: {
  md: 'clamp(1rem, 0.9rem + 0.5cqi, 1.25rem)',
}
```

Because it's lazy substitution, this naturally nests correctly: same font-size token would resolve against the outer content container everywhere *except* inside something like `PlayerCard`, which establishes its own nearer `container-type` context — so it'd get its own local scale there instead. That's probably desirable, not a bug, but worth being deliberate about since there's no way to override which ancestor container a `cqi` unit binds to (no equivalent of named containers for the *unit itself*, only for `@container` at-rules).

Important gotcha either way: per spec, `cqi`/`cqw` compute to a definite `0` (not an error) when there's no container-context ancestor at all — e.g. a portalled modal, or a page that bypasses `CustomAppShell`. So any theme value using `cqi` must be written as `clamp(rem-floor, rem-floor + N·cqi, rem-ceiling)`, never bare `Ncqi`, so it degrades to the rem floor instead of vanishing to zero-size text.

## Also unresolved

- `clamp()` sizing (viewport- or container-relative) gives continuous fluid scaling but isn't a first-class Mantine concept — it's just a raw CSS string Mantine passes through untouched, no theme/breakpoint integration.
- Mantine's own responsive `fz={{ base, sm, ... }}` object syntax is the documented, first-class idiom — but only for viewport-relative sizing.
- Need to decide whether "container queries for the whole content area" is worth the added conceptual overhead (one more wrapper, one more thing to remember on every new page) versus just living with the viewport/content-width mismatch, which may be minor in practice.

It captures: the viewport-vs-content-width mismatch caused by the nav bar, the existing container-query usage (`PlayerCard`, `CountryFlag`, etc.) as the working precedent, the sketch of moving `AppShell.Main` itself to a container context, the theme-level `cqi` idea with its lazy-resolution/nesting behaviour, the zero-fallback gotcha, and the open question of whether it's worth the added overhead — all flagged as unresolved, not a decision.

That doesn't give me enough to act on — did you mean to type something else?

Is there a Mantine way to express             style={{ overflow: 'hidden' }}
?

input.command: find node_modules/@mantine/core -iname "*style-props*" -name "*.d.ts" | xargs grep -l "overflow" 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; grep -rn "overflow" node_modules/@mantine/core/lib/core/Box/style-props/ 2>/dev/null | head -20
input.description: Check whether Mantine's style-props system supports an overflow prop

content.text:
No — that's the full list of Mantine's style props (`m`, `p`, `bd`, `bg`, `w`/`h`, `pos`, `display`, `flex`, etc.), and `overflow` isn't one of them, confirmed straight from `style-props.types.ts` in the installed package.

So `style={{ overflow: 'hidden' }}` is exactly right per the project convention: CLAUDE.md says to prefer Mantine style props but fall back to raw `style`/CSS "when no Mantine style prop covers the property needed" — this is that case.
